skill.name: when-setting-network-security-use-network-security-setup
skill.description: Configure Claude Code network isolation guidance with trusted domains, access policies, firewall examples, and validation steps.
skill.summary: Plan and document network access controls for Claude Code sandbox environments.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: The high weak-cryptography alerts are false positives caused by words such as description and Design. The skill is documentation for network security setup, with no evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent. It remains medium risk because its shell examples can modify firewall defaults, /etc/hosts, and system environment files if executed on a real host.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Firewall Examples Can Disrupt Host Networking
medium_findings.description: The generated setup script applies default DROP policies for INPUT and OUTPUT traffic and allows only selected ports. This is legitimate network hardening guidance, but running it on a host or shared runner can break package access, remote sessions, and application connectivity without rollback controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly create and execute a shell script that changes iptables policies. The risk is operational disruption rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: System Network Configuration Writes Need Review
medium_findings.description: The setup script appends entries to /etc/hosts and writes /etc/environment.d/network-security.conf. These persistent system changes can alter DNS and proxy behavior beyond the current task if applied without administrator review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands target system configuration paths directly. The values are not malicious, but the persistence and privilege requirements create clear deployment risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography detections point to ordinary prose and labels such as description, Design, and design. No cryptographic algorithm use, key handling, encryption code, or hashing implementation was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these locations contain documentation words, not crypto primitives. The repeated alerts are substring matches.

low_findings.title: Outbound Network Test Uses Fixed Domains
low_findings.description: The test script uses curl against api.anthropic.com and malicious.com to validate allow and deny behavior. It does not transmit secrets or dynamic data, but users should replace the blocked-domain test with a controlled internal domain.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and fixed, with no credential access or data upload. External network contact still requires user awareness.

low_findings.title: Email Capability Alert Is A Blocked Protocol Reference
low_findings.description: The SMTP mention appears only in a list of blocked protocols. No email-sending command, SMTP client, credential use, or message construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context explicitly marks SMTP as blocked. There is no supporting code path for sending email.

dangerous_patterns.title: Default-Deny iptables Policy Without Rollback
dangerous_patterns.description: The example sets INPUT and OUTPUT policies to DROP before adding a small allowlist. This can immediately cut off needed connectivity if run outside an isolated test environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The iptables commands are explicit and privileged. The file does not include rollback, dry-run, or environment checks near the commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent System File Modification
dangerous_patterns.description: The script writes host and environment configuration under /etc. These changes can persist after the skill task and affect other tools or users on the machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The target paths and heredoc writes are clear. The values are understandable, but the scope is system-wide.

dangerous_patterns.title: Quick Start Invokes Generated Shell Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start instructs users to run generated network configuration and test scripts. That is expected for the skill, but it raises risk because the scripts perform privileged network changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The README directly references script execution. The risk depends on the generated script contents, which include firewall and system configuration changes.

content.value_statement: Network access in coding sandboxes can become too broad or poorly documented. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through trusted domains, firewall policies, tests, and deployment notes.
actual_capabilities: Documents required external domains, protocols, ports, and rate limits.
Creates a sample whitelist network policy for common developer services.
Provides shell examples for firewall rules, hosts entries, and environment variables.
Adds test steps for trusted domain access, blocked domain checks, and local port checks.
Produces deployment notes covering trusted domains, monitoring logs, and maintenance tasks.
limitations: Examples require administrator review before use on shared or production systems.
The skill does not detect the current host firewall or operating system automatically.
Rollback, backup, and dry-run commands are not included in the provided examples.
Domain and port allowlists must be adapted to each organization.

use_cases.title: Sandbox Access Review
use_cases.description: Map the domains and ports a Claude Code workspace needs before enabling outbound access.

use_cases.title: Developer Environment Hardening
use_cases.description: Create a documented allowlist for package registries, source control, and model API endpoints.

use_cases.title: Policy Documentation
use_cases.description: Generate clear deployment and maintenance notes for network controls around AI coding tools.

prompt_templates.title: List Network Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new workspace
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to list the domains, ports, and protocols my Claude Code sandbox needs. Keep the first version conservative.

prompt_templates.title: Draft An Allowlist Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a trusted-domain network policy for GitHub, package registries, Docker Hub, and model API access.

prompt_templates.title: Review Before Deployment
prompt_templates.scenario: Check operational risk before running commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review the firewall and environment changes before deployment. Identify rollback steps and unsafe assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Customize Enterprise Controls
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced adaptation for a governed environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to adapt the network security plan for our enterprise proxy, private package mirrors, and restricted outbound egress.

output_examples.input: Create a network access plan for a Claude Code workspace that uses GitHub and Python packages.
output: A short requirements document listing GitHub, PyPI, HTTPS, SSH, and local application ports.
A recommended whitelist policy with denied protocols and monitoring notes.
A warning to review firewall commands before running them on the host.

output_examples.input: Help me test whether my sandbox blocks untrusted domains.
output: A test checklist for trusted API access, blocked destination checks, and local port validation.
Guidance to use a controlled blocked-domain endpoint instead of an unrelated public domain.
A reminder to capture logs for failed and allowed connections.

output_examples.input: Prepare deployment notes for network isolation changes.
output: A concise deployment sequence covering review, apply, test, monitor, and maintain steps.
A list of files and logs administrators should verify after rollout.
A maintenance cadence for domain review and policy testing.

best_practices: Review all firewall and system file changes before executing any generated script.
Test policies in an isolated environment before applying them to developer machines.
Keep trusted domains, ports, and proxy settings documented with an owner and review date.
anti_patterns: Running default-deny firewall commands over an active remote session without rollback access.
Copying public example domains into production policies without organization approval.
Treating generated network policies as a replacement for security team review.

faq.question: Does this skill run network commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and command examples that a user may choose to run.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Use it as planning material only until an administrator reviews and tests every command.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but its examples can change firewall and system network configuration.

faq.question: Can I use it with enterprise proxies?
faq.answer: Yes, but proxy variables and trusted domains must be customized for your environment.

faq.question: Does it include rollback steps?
faq.answer: No. Add backups and rollback commands before applying any firewall or system file changes.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
